result:
The Persistence of Small Joys

There's a particular quality to morning light in late September that I've never been able to name properly. It arrives later than summer's early brightness, slanting through windows with a golden heaviness that makes dust motes visible and somehow important. I noticed it again last Tuesday, standing in my kitchen waiting for coffee, and felt that familiar surge of something between gratitude and melancholy—that recognition of beauty that contains within it the awareness of its passing.

I've been thinking lately about how we survive the long stretches of ordinary time. Not the crises, not the mountaintop moments, but the Tuesday afternoons, the emails to return, the dishes accumulating in the sink. The novelist Dorothy Whipple called it "the beauty of the commonplace," and I've been trying to practice seeing it, though practice is perhaps the wrong word, suggesting effort where what I mean is closer to surrender.

My grandmother kept a garden for sixty years in the same patch of Ohio clay. She grew tomatoes, mostly, and peppers, and marigolds she planted around the borders because some magazine in 1973 told her they deterred pests. Whether they worked or not, she never stopped planting them. I think now she loved the orange. I think she loved having something to tend. When her arthritis made kneeling impossible, she gardened from a kitchen stool she dragged between the rows. When she could no longer manage the stool, she directed operations from a lawn chair, my grandfather or one of the neighbors executing her vision with varying degrees of precision. The marigolds persisted. The tomatoes grew crooked without her hands to stake them properly, but they grew.

I keep a small garden now, nothing like hers, just some herbs in pots and a single tomato plant that produced three fruits this year before succumbing to blight. I felt disproportionately sad about it, standing in the drizzle with the withered vine in my hands, and then laughed at myself, and then felt sad again in a different way—sad for my grandmother gone ten years now, sad for the particular loneliness of middle age when you realize how much you didn't ask while there was still someone to answer.

The writer Ross Gay spent a year noting delights and collected them into a book that became unexpectedly popular, as if we were all waiting for permission to find our own. I read it on a plane, crying over his description of carrying a tomato through an airport, and the man next to me asked if I was alright in that wary way people have, and I said yes, which was true and not true in the way that things often are. I was alright because I was feeling something precisely. The tomato mattered. The carrying of it mattered. The stranger's awkward kindness in asking mattered too.

I've been trying to carry things more carefully. Not just tomatoes. Conversations. The weight of other people's days, which they sometimes set down in front of you without warning, the way a cat deposits a dead thing on the doorstep—look, this is what I hunted, this is what I carry. The skill is in receiving it without recoiling, in understanding that the offering is trust, not burden, though it is also burden.

Music helps. I've been listening to Bach's cello suites again, the way the notes seem to both mourn and insist on continuing, which is perhaps the essential human sound. Casals recorded them in his eighties, by which time he had lived through two world wars, exile, the loss of almost everything familiar. He still practiced every morning. When asked why, he said something about the birds in the trees near his window in Puerto Rico, about feeling the obligation to begin again. I don't fully understand this but I feel I understand it, the way you sometimes recognize truth before you can articulate it.

The other day I found a letter I'd written in college, printed out from an email account that no longer exists, saved in a box of papers I was sorting through during a fit of organizational ambition. The letter was to a friend who died five years later, too young, from something preventable that he didn't prevent. Reading my own words from that distance was strange—how earnest I was, how certain of things I no longer believe, how uncertain of things that now seem obvious. I wanted to warn him, and couldn't. I wanted to warn myself, and couldn't. The letter existed in a space without consequences, already written, already received, already part of the past that determines the present without allowing revision.

I kept it. I keep most things, which is its own kind of practice, its own kind of faith. The belief that meaning accumulates, that patterns emerge if you wait long enough, that the marigolds matter even if the pests come anyway.

Evening now. The light is fading in that particular autumn way, the blue deepening toward something almost purple at the edges of the sky. I can hear my neighbor practicing piano, scales ascending and descending, the occasional stumble and restart. She's learning, I think, or relearning. The imperfect repetition is itself a kind of beauty, the willingness to begin again, to hit the wrong note and continue, to trust that the right one will come or that the wrong one will become right through context and persistence.

I think this is what I wanted to say, though I started not knowing. That we continue. That the ordinary contains its own astonishments if we don't rush past them. That the tomato carried through the airport is a small miracle of attention. That my grandmother's marigolds outlived her, briefly, and then didn't, and that this is not failure but simply the nature of tending, the nature of love, the nature of time which takes everything and gives everything in the same motion, like breathing, like scales played imperfectly in the evening, ascending toward something we can't yet hear but keep practicing anyway.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
